description:
Goal: the semantic loop should not spend time analyzing impossible activations that never survive into the closed frontier. Signal: the quicksort trace no longer analyzes `qsort([])`, `qsort(none)`, `append(none, ...)`, `dbg(none)`-style dead islands, while the final closed frontier stays the same and `CheckSemanticClosure` / `AnalyzeActivation` counts fall materially. Strategy: make activation admission and call propagation respect inhabitation and subsumption at the Compiler2 boundary, then tighten the root walk so only live frontier changes reopen semantic work.

Change:
- Revisit activation-key admission and propagation so bottom/empty transient shapes do not become ordinary semantic work unless semantically real.
- Tighten `CheckSemanticClosure` / `AnalyzeActivation` interaction around changed inputs and live waits only.
- Preserve the current closed quicksort and `Enum.reduce` frontiers while reducing rerun churn.

Why:
- The current trace shows real work being spent on dead-end semantic islands that do not survive into the settled root closure.

Checks:
- Quicksort trace job counts drop without changing the final closed activation/executable frontier.
- No dead transient activations remain in the trace unless the rooted program semantically requires them.
title: Compiler2 fixpoint: prune dead transient activations and reduce root-walk churn
